title: Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age
caption:
Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age

Percentage of children at lower secondary school age meeting reading comprehension standards. This data accounts for school completion rates to estimate reading skills for all children, not just those in school.

The bar chart displays the share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age in 2022, sorted from highest to lowest. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to 80%, with horizontal grid lines marking 20-point intervals. Ireland leads the distribution with a value exceeding 80%, followed closely by several other nations that remain above the 70% mark. A large cluster of countries, including Australia, Lithuania, and Spain, falls within the 60% to 75% range. The bars are color-coded by income level, with dark green representing high-income countries, light green for upper-middle-income, light purple for lower-middle-income, and dark purple for low-income countries. High-income nations dominate the left side of the chart, generally maintaining proficiency rates above 50%. As the values decrease moving rightward, the composition shifts to include more upper-middle-income countries like Moldova and Brazil, which sit between 40% and 50%. Further down the scale, values drop below 30%, featuring countries such as Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan. The lowest recorded values appear on the far right, where Cambodia and neighboring entries fall below 20%. Lower-middle-income countries are visible in the lower quartile of the chart, while no low-income countries are explicitly labeled in the visible selection. The gradient of bar heights illustrates a wide disparity in reading proficiency outcomes across the sampled nations.
